nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef flats, coral reefs and seagrass beds over sand and rubble substrates.

decorPreferences: Mature live rock with abundant microfauna and biofilm
Rocky hiding places and open grazing surfaces

diet.notes: In the wild grazes on biofilms, bacterial mats, microalgae, sponges and detritus across mature reef surfaces. This diet is notoriously difficult to replicate in captivity, and most specimens are believed to slowly starve over months. Has been reported to accept tiny pieces of clam or specialty starfish foods placed beneath the disc, though many individuals refuse all offered foods.

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by dr.scott.mills, 3968x2976. Tack-sharp in-situ shot of a whole 5-armed individual in vibrant classic royal blue on a pale sandy reef flat — clearly shows the smooth cylindrical arms and uniform electric-blue coloration.
Photo by Diego Delso, 6301x4201. Sharp high-resolution in-situ image of a whole rich-blue star sprawled over a colorful reef; strong diagnostic color and full body in frame.
Photo by Joao D'Andretta, 2048x1448. Deep-blue whole individual on a reef ledge with open-water backdrop — sharp, correct ID, adds compositional variety to the set.

summary: The Blue Linckia Sea Star is a striking tropical species (Linckia laevigata) that is notoriously difficult to keep — most specimens slowly starve in captivity. Best reserved for mature, food-rich reef tanks managed by experienced specialists.
careNotes: The Blue Linckia Sea Star is one of the most widely sold yet most poorly suited sea stars for the average home aquarium. It feeds on a specialised diet of bacterial films, microalgae and sponges grazed from mature reef surfaces, and most captive specimens are reported to die within 6–12 months from slow starvation despite appearing healthy on arrival. It should only be considered for very mature reef aquariums (generally advised at 12+ months of established live rock) with extensive microfauna and stable, pristine water chemistry. A very slow drip acclimation of at least 2–3 hours is generally advised, and the specimen should never be exposed to air during transfer. The species is highly sensitive to swings in salinity, pH, oxygen and temperature, and is completely intolerant of copper-based medications. Watch for the small parasitic snail Thyca crystallina, which attaches near the base of the arms.
breedingNotes: Has not been bred in captivity in any sustainable way. Wild reproduction is broadcast spawning; Linckia species are also capable of asexual reproduction through autotomy (arm fragmentation regenerating into a new individual, the basis for the 'comet' trade name), but this is rarely observed in home aquariums.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary care reference. Notes the species is Expert-Only, copper-intolerant, requires drip acclimation, and lists temp 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, SG 1.023–1.025. Listed adult size of '1 inch' clearly refers to a juvenile/disc size and was discarded in favour of the 20–30 cm cross-source figure.

additional.name: Reef2Reef Wiki
additional.notes: Assigned secondary source returned HTTP 403. Swapped to alternative secondaries below.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Linckia laevigata
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Ophidiasteridae, Valvatida), native range, depth, and confirmation of ~30 cm adult diameter.

additional.name: Reeflings — Linckia laevigata Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (24–27 °C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.026) and high water-stability sensitivity. Tank-size recommendation of 200 L was treated as a low-end outlier; we biased upward per playbook.

additional.name: Reefapp Encyclopedia — Linckia laevigata
additional.notes: Confirmed difficulty (described as 'hard to keep alive and thriving'), 30 cm adult size, and a more conservative 600 L minimum tank recommendation. Used to justify rounding tank size up to 400 L as a mature-reef target.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Notoriously high captive mortality — most specimens slowly starve within 6–12 months because their specialised biofilm/microbial-mat diet is extremely difficult to replicate. Suitable only for very mature, food-rich reef systems maintained by experienced reef-keepers.
